@Green Music : the way I approach negotiations is to say that I've done extensive research about your levels and know the comp details. And that I'm targeting level X and at that point they should make me their best offer. And that I'm not going to state a number right now. 
The challenge with saying that range is - what if they give it to you at the 30% mark? Technically you got what you asked for, but will leave money on the table?

That makes sense. I think I just made so many mistakes my first go around and one was to tell the recruiter a number (which was pretty low) during our first chat. She never called, we just did everything over linked in chat and I figured I’d be in the same situation again. messages.content: @AG How does the Wall of Fame/Shame work? It sounds interesting.

works as a positive or negative reinforcement I guess ? 
hall of fame : stack rank the teams who are doing well and call out the top 3 in some report you send out to a mass audience. 
hall of shame : in addition call out the bottom 5, but maybe to a smaller leadership audience. 
ofcourse, this may not work in all org cultures, there is always the risk of collateral damage, etc. so make sure your assessment criteria is objective and clearly published, etc.

Yeah, this could go very wrong. Hard work people could get demotivated if they don't get on the fame wall. People will also try to cover up their mistakes or failures to avoid getting on the shame wall. 
Although, I guess this could be fun to use for lighter goals.

yeah, YMMV 🤷🏻‍♂️ . a Bug SLA dashboard for example is an objective Wall of fame/shame. Sure, you have to build the right guardrails to make sure people don't game the system and simply close issues or don't report them at all. I've had successes in some places, not so much in others

We used to do it with customers. We'd show a customer a chart with all of their competitors (without their names) doing better in an area we wanted them to improve. It's an efficient tool if used correctly
Interesting…but yeah the collateral damage is true as well.
@AG  anything else you tried and doing for the accountability part
well you've got to figure out the "what is in it for me" part. if folks are not incentivized (or the other side, penalized) to demonstrate certain behavior, it'll be hard to influence anything.

Up-leveling the shame game.  Make the bottom 20% go through a structured process improvement technique and report out to leadership. When the potential bandwidth drain of the task exceeds that of the ask, the ask gets done. (Not recommended, but I have seen it/ lived through it)
We do a bit similar, we have a recogintion okatform where  emoloyees can recognize co-workers and we reward the  top 3 recognizers with a small gift in monthly staff meetings to have that push

Does anyone have experience pitching and landing a TPM position in a company that is not looking for that role? I came across a startup in an industry that I'm passionate about. They are hiring for a product manager and have a small engineering team. I'd love to bring my skillset to their company but at the same time not make it a career-limiting move. From The Pragmatic Engineer's latest blog. 
> 5. Introducing the TPM role in an organization
> 
> At which stage in a company’s growth is it worth considering hiring the first TPM? Here is how to identify the right time, according to Yixin Zhu, senior manager of tech programs at Tempo:
> 
> ‘Even though TPMs are often associated with process, any good TPM actually has a healthy distaste for process. TPMs don’t want to be process managers. There’s a wide spectrum of TPM. It’s often the weaker TPMs who reach immediately for processes as a way to fix things. 
> 
> ‘However, there’s a healthy yin and yang balance between leaning on processes, and leaning into relationships. When you’re a small company, you can lean on relationships to solve a problem, walking up to people and solving things directly with them. However, as the company grows, the web of relationships also grows and solving problems just based on relationships starts to break down.
> 
> ‘The time to bring in the first TPMs is typically when you lean too heavily on relationships, but this approach is starting to fail. It’s even better to bring in TPMs when you’re not yet failing, but you can foresee that you need people to manage the transition from engineering running from a relationship-only model, to one where there are some lightweight processes in place.
> 
> ‘We’re not talking about a one-time transition, but a rolling transition with microclimates. By microclimates I mean how you might be a mature organization, but within it you could incubate a startup, where this process starts over again. This startup within the company works fine purely based on relationships, but needs TPM support as it passes a certain stage.
> 
> ‘Introducing the TPM role is all about balance. There is no one solution for your problems. For example, for several organization-wide problems you could have the alternatives of automating processes, buying a vendor solution, hiring dedicated people for certain areas, or bringing in a TPM. However, if I were to focus on one signal, I’d look at the stage where you need to balance out relationships with some process. This is your cue to think about TPMs.’
> 
> I asked Sophia Vicent, senior director of TPM at DoorDash the same question; when is it a good time to hire the first TPM?
> 
> 'Consider introducing the role when leadership sees gaps in execution and communication. You’ll start to see engineering execution slow down because lots of projects are happening across teams and more coordination is needed. You might see communication gaps between teams that contribute to this, especially with remote settings. When you start to see these cracks, is when you should start to consider bringing in TPMs.
> 
> Which ratio of TPMs-to-engineers should you aim for? Sophia Vicent discussed this topic in-depth in her article Six principles for building a world-class TPM team. In it she writes:
> 
>     'Although ratios are a terrible measure for a small engineering organization, they become a reasonable way to plan hiring and headcount as engineering becomes a bigger organization. My target ratio of engineer to TPM at scale is approximately 50 to 1. I also ensure that each engineering team they embed with understands the value they bring and gives them an opportunity to be part of strategic as well as tactical execution and planning.'
> 
> At what engineering org size should you bring on your first TPM? Sophia shared her observation that it’s rare for a company under 60-70 engineers to have TPMs. At Uber, the first TPM was hired when there were 130 engineers, and this felt too late because the first TPM hires were soon drowning in work. Each company’s situation is different, but these numbers, together with observing the gaps in execution, might help pinpoint when it’s a good time to introduce this role.

Re: recall a question from the end of the zoom chat, it mentioned a question about TPM/PM IC transition to people manager when managing teams of ~20.   

It certainly doesn't hurt to have that experience, I believe you gain a lot of good experience with having 1:1s with the team, learning about the team members, how to help motivate them, leading without authority (these are the people manager traits that are key).  

There might be a gap in the area of actual people manager duties (e.g. performance reviews, hiring/firing, HR relations to name a few areas); but within your current org if you have the trust of those responsible for the manager role decisions, you could put yourself in a good position to be moved from an IC to people manager.

Thank you all for joining the chat today and posting excellent questions (nobody asked about the stock?!). A few notes:

- Program management role but we look for XFN skills, risk management, structuring programs. We really are looking for a balance of all of these vectors. 
Culture fit and behavioral assessment are very important as well. 
Interviews: 
- Tech round - domain knowledge and expertise 
- Solution/System Design - how we do build automation and SW or asset management system 
- Program sense - good core fundamental PM skills 
- Working with partners/stakeholders, communication, working without authority 
- Behavioral - move fast, be bold, work well with others, direct (but not disrespectful)
- >IC6 - leadership interviews - a little program sense and XFN + decision making skills (show poor decisions you’ve made) 
TPM seniority is judged by the scope/scale of the work. Can the individual identify the opportunity, get others aligned to join and execution? Junior TPMs execute well in a program assigned to them and are more tactical. 

- Career planning: focus on working the job you want and have strength in, not the near term tactical (like promo, comp). Build strong relationship, it’s a small industry. Focus on the long game 

30-60-90 day planning: 
- Meta is special - we let people ramp. No rush to make decisions. Understand the culture and how things are done. Learn, absorb. 
- 30 days - learn learn learn. Start doing small things - take notes, make work posts as a means to learn. Use your fresh set of eyes as an advantage, ask questions and challenge the status quo 
- 60 days - start taking the lead more, organize events 
- 90 days - have an assertive point of view; ‘here is what we should be doing’

Answering few of the questions from the event
Q: In the current market is it advisable to look out or should we hang on till market stabilizes
A: If you are not in a hurry and are learning and have interesting things to do, and your company is stable, you could wait out a few months. However, there is no harm in keeping a tab on the job openings and go for something you like. You never know what might just work out. As mentioned day, just do your due diligence on the new company.


Q:I joined my company at the beginning of the year, like everyone my RSUs are getting crushed. So I've started my job search earlier than expected. Do HM care about how long you have been at a company? Are many companies offering compensation for lost RSUs?
A: Some companies do provide additional comp for lost RSUs but most don’t. Even Meta did not when their stock took a dip due to Cambridge Analytica.  Some companies/recrutiers do question if you are changing jobs too often or just looking within a few months of joining. You need to have an answer ready that is not just about the RSU value. Eventually, if the RSUs getting crushed is affected your living, then it makes sense that you are looking for new role - but you may want to assess for the long term. There are more factors than just the RSU that would make your job interesting. 

Q. How do you find a good org match when everyone utilizes their TPMs so differently?
A: There are two things here - 1 - what aspects of the TPM role do you enjoy and need to have in your job. And 2 - Ask the HM and other interviewers about the culture, view of TPM, interactions etc so you get a sense whether they utilize the TPM that aligns with your values. You should absolutely look for the right fit.

Hey @everyone!  This week we'll have a Lean Coffee TPM session.  Here's some details on what to expect from this week's session (Friday Jul 21st at 3pm EST - invite above):

**TPM Lean Coffee**

We're excited to have you in our upcoming Lean Coffee session! In case you're not familiar with it, Lean Coffee is a structured, yet agenda-less meeting format that allows for open and democratic discussions. Here's how our virtual session will work:

**Tools: ** We will use Zoom for our video and voice communication, and Mural for our virtual whiteboard. Please make sure you have access to both platforms. Links will be sent to you before the meeting.  (zoom link is in the invite above)

**Format: **The session is broken down into the following steps:

1. **Setting the Stage:** At the start of the meeting, we'll do a brief round of introductions and set out the ground rules of Lean Coffee. You'll be introduced to the structure and flow of the session.

2. **Topic Generation:** Each participant will be asked to think of some topics they'd like to discuss. These topics are added to the Mural board as individual sticky notes under the 'To Discuss' column. These can be any issues, ideas, or questions you want to explore.

3. **Voting:** Once all topics have been added to the Mural board, you will vote on the ones you find most interesting. Each participant gets two votes. You can use both votes on one topic or spread them across two topics. The topics with the most votes are moved to the 'Discussing' column.

4. **Discussion: **We'll start the timer (usually set for 5-8 minutes) and begin discussing the highest-voted topic. Everyone is invited to share their thoughts openly and freely.

5. **Thumbs Up/Thumbs Down:** When the timer ends, participants will give a 'thumbs up' if they want to continue the discussion, or a 'thumbs down' if they are ready to move on. Majority vote rules. If the discussion continues, the timer is reset. If not, we move to the next highest-voted topic.

6. **Wrap Up:** At the end of the meeting, we'll briefly summarize the outcomes and discuss how the session went. Your feedback is crucial to us for improving future Lean Coffee sessions!

The key objective of a Lean Coffee session is not to resolve all issues or completely exhaust each topic. Instead, it's about fostering rich, democratic discussions, promoting learning, and unearthing innovative ideas. We look forward to your active participation.

See you at the Lean Coffee session!

Tips for job search from today's chat: 

General: 
- Prepare for interviews with peer/mock interview
- Check LinkedIn group and hiring channel here on discord 
- Network with ex colleagues and friends, let people know you are looking for a job.  
- Build your brand and increase your exposure on LI: post stories, books you read, etc. 
- Learn on AI, LLMs, GenAI. Demand is higher in these spaces. 

Resume: 
- Short resume with datapoints oriented around scope and impact.
- Run your resume through automated screen tools 
- Make sure the complexity of the programs you worked on shows through in your resume. 

Applying: 
- AI based job application submissions (Mario will share links) 
- Chrome plugin to match LinkedIn profile to email + share your cover letter (don't spam!)
- Be open for remote opportunities (e.g. github)
- Leverage referrals, they get more attention. 
- Apply to jobs you match well with: quality over quantity. Double down about your specialties.
- Consider Product Management positions, there is more demand in that space these days. But keep in mind that many experienced PMs are also looking for jobs.

Hey all, we noticed a decrease in attendance in TPM chats toward the end of last year so I would like to  we'd like to people's interest in resuming these chats.

**:bar_chart: 📊 Here are a few topics I came up with completely by myself with ChatGPT. Please vote if any of these look interesting for us to discuss in an upcoming TPM chat.  Feel free to vote for more than one option. **

embeds.description:
🇦 The Evolution of TPM Metrics: Beyond Delivery to Value Measurement;      Discussion Points:         How to align program success metrics with business outcomes.         Balancing traditional KPIs (on-time delivery, budget adherence) with value-focused metrics (customer impact, revenue contribution).         Tools and techniques for real-time metrics tracking and reporting.
🇧 Navigating Hybrid and Remote Work in Complex Programs;      Discussion Points:         Strategies for managing cross-functional teams in hybrid and remote environments.         Tools and techniques to enhance collaboration and track progress without micromanaging.         Addressing the challenges of time zone differences and cultural nuances in global teams.
🇨 Upskilling for the Evolving TPM Role;      Discussion Points:         Emerging skills TPMs need to thrive in industries like cloud computing, cybersecurity, or Web3.         The growing importance of technical depth vs. breadth in program management.         Recommended certifications, courses, and resources for career advancement.
🇩 Risk Management in High-Stakes, High-Visibility Programs;      Discussion Points:         Identifying and mitigating risks in fast-paced, high-impact projects.         Communicating risk effectively to non-technical stakeholders.         Lessons learned from high-profile program failures or successes.
🇪 Thriving in the TPM Job Market: Preparing for Job Changes and Interviews;      Discussion Points:         Current trends in the TPM job market and the skills most in demand.         Strategies for effective resume writing and LinkedIn optimization.         Common interview questions and how to showcase technical and leadership skills.
